front: Define a tree in graph theory
back: A tree is a connected acyclic graph (graph with no cycles). Alternatively, it is a graph where there exists exactly one simple path between every pair of vertices.

front: What is the relationship between vertices and edges in a tree?
back: A tree with n vertices has exactly n-1 edges. This is a fundamental property of all trees.

front: What is a spanning tree of a graph?
back: A spanning tree is a subgraph that is a tree and includes all vertices of the original graph. It is a maximal acyclic subgraph.
In a rooted tree, one vertex is designated as the root, and all other vertices have a unique parent along the unique path to the root. This introduces hierarchy in the tree.

front: What is a leaf in a tree?
back: A leaf (or terminal vertex) is a vertex of degree 1 in a tree. Every tree with at least two vertices has at least two leaves.
A binary tree is a rooted tree where each vertex has at most two children, typically called left child and right child.

front: What is a forest in graph theory?
back: A forest is a disjoint union of trees. It is an acyclic graph that may be disconnected (each connected component is a tree).

front: What is the center of a tree?
back: The center of a tree is obtained by repeatedly removing all leaves until one or two vertices remain. These remaining vertices form the center.
Tree traversal is the process of visiting all vertices of a tree in a systematic order. Common methods: Pre-order, In-order, and Post-order traversals.

front: What is a complete binary tree?
back: A complete binary tree is a binary tree where all levels except possibly the last are completely filled, and all vertices are as far left as possible.

front: Define the height of a tree
back: The height of a tree is the length of the longest path from the root to a leaf. For an empty tree, height is -1 or 0 depending on convention.

front: What is an internal vertex in a tree?
back: An internal vertex (or internal node) is a vertex that has at least one child, i.e., degree ≥ 2 in a rooted tree (except the root if it has degree 1).
A weighted tree is a tree where each edge has an associated weight (or cost). Used in network routing and MST algorithms.
Two trees are isomorphic if there exists a one-to-one correspondence between their vertices that preserves adjacency. Structural equivalence.

front: What is a directed acyclic graph (DAG)?
back: A DAG is a directed graph with no directed cycles. Unlike trees, DAGs can have multiple paths between vertices and share substructures.
